A cleanup LLM (D) adds cost and a new failure point. More prompt instructions (A) improve things but can't guarantee schema conformance.

exercises.question: In a validation-retry loop, what should happen when the maximum retry count is reached without producing valid output?
options: Silently return an empty result
Return the last invalid output anyway — something is better than nothing
Retry indefinitely until valid output is produced
Escalate to a human reviewer with the validation errors and all attempted outputs, so they can correct or make a judgment call
exercises.explanation: When automated correction fails, escalate to a human. Returning invalid output (A) pushes bad data downstream. Empty results (C) hide the failure. Infinite retries (D) waste resources. Human escalation with full context (validation errors + all attempts) enables informed decision-making.

exercises.question: Your structured output schema requires a `risk_score` field that's an integer between 1-10. Claude consistently produces valid JSON, but the risk scores cluster around 5-7 and don't differentiate between clearly high-risk and low-risk items. The schema validation passes (1-10 is valid). How do you improve the output quality?
options: Widen the range to 1-100 for more granularity
Add a validation rule that rejects scores between 4-7 to force differentiation
Switch from numeric scores to categorical labels (Low/Medium/High/Critical)

Add explicit scoring criteria to the prompt: 'Score 1-3 for items with no security impact, 4-6 for moderate impact with mitigations available, 7-9 for high impact requiring immediate attention, 10 for critical unmitigated vulnerabilities.' Include few-shot examples with scores at each end of the range
exercises.explanation: The problem is semantic, not structural — the schema validates fine but the output lacks discrimination. Explicit scoring criteria with calibrating examples solve this by defining what each score range means in concrete terms. Widening the range (A) doesn't help if Claude still clusters. Rejecting middle scores (C) is arbitrary. Categories (D) lose granularity. This is a case where the prompt complements the schema.

lessons.title: Batch API & Cost Optimization
lessons.description: Architect cost-efficient systems using the Message Batches API for non-urgent workloads, with structured custom_ids and hybrid real-time/batch strategies.

sections.content:
Batch API & Cost Optimization

The Message Batches API processes multiple requests at 50% cost savings with up to 24-hour completion windows. Understanding when to use batch vs. real-time is an architectural decision.

Message Batches API

  • Submit a batch of up to 10,000 message requests
  • Each request has a custom_id for tracking
  • Results are processed within 24 hours
  • 50% cost reduction vs. standard API pricing
  • No streaming — results are available when the batch completes

When to Use Batch

Nightly code analysis runs, weekly documentation generation, bulk data processing that isn't time-sensitive, training data preparation, large-scale content generation, any workload where 24-hour SLA is acceptable.

When NOT to Use Batch

Real-time user interactions, PR-blocking checks, interactive coding sessions, urgent incident response, any workflow where latency matters.

custom_id Pattern

Each request gets a custom_id string (max 64 chars). Best practice: Use structured IDs like review_pr-123_file-utils.ts so you can parse the ID to understand what the result is for.

Cost Architecture

For organizations running Claude at scale: identify which workloads can tolerate 24h latency, route those to batch for 50% savings, keep real-time for user-facing and time-sensitive workflows, monitor batch completion times and adjust if needed.

exercises.question: Your company runs Claude-powered code review on every PR (real-time) and a nightly security scan across all repositories (200 repos, ~50 files each). Monthly API cost is $15,000 — $5,000 for PR reviews and $10,000 for nightly scans. How would you optimize costs?
options: Move everything to the Batch API for maximum savings
Move PR reviews to batch and keep nightly scans on real-time
Keep PR reviews on the standard API (real-time) and move nightly security scans to the Batch API, saving ~$5,000/month (50% of the $10,000 scan cost)
Reduce the number of repositories scanned to cut costs
exercises.explanation: PR reviews need real-time responses (they block merge). Nightly scans already run overnight and can easily tolerate 24-hour SLA. Moving $10,000 of scan workload to batch saves $5,000/month. Moving PR reviews to batch (A, D) would delay merge feedback. Reducing scan coverage (C) weakens security.

exercises.question: What is the maximum number of requests in a single Message Batch?
options: Unlimited
exercises.explanation: The Message Batches API supports up to 10,000 requests per batch. Each request has its own custom_id and can have different prompts, models, and parameters. For workloads larger than 10,000 items, submit multiple batches.

exercises.question: You're designing a batch job that reviews 5,000 code files nightly. Each file needs a separate API request. You need to correlate each review result back to its file and repository. What is the BEST custom_id strategy?
options: Use structured IDs that encode the context: 'review_repo-myapp_file-src/utils.ts_date-2025-03-15'
Use sequential numbers: '1', '2', '3', ... '5000'
Don't set custom_ids — let the API assign them automatically
Use random UUIDs for each request
exercises.explanation: Structured IDs let you parse the ID to understand what each result is for — which repo, which file, which date. This is essential for correlating 5,000 results back to their source files. Sequential numbers (A) require a separate lookup table. Random UUIDs (C) are unparseable. The API doesn't auto-assign (D) — custom_id is required.

lessons.title: Multi-Pass Review Workflows
lessons.description: Design multi-pass review patterns (generate-review-revise, specialist review, adversarial review) for high-stakes content generation.

sections.content:
Multi-Pass Review Workflows

Multi-pass review is a workflow pattern where content passes through multiple review stages, each catching different types of issues.

Why Multi-Pass

A single-pass review tends to focus on the most obvious issues and miss subtle ones. By running multiple focused passes, you improve coverage and accuracy.

Common Multi-Pass Patterns

  1. Generate -> Review -> Revise: Pass 1 generates the initial content, Pass 2 reviews against explicit criteria, Pass 3 revises based on review feedback.
  2. Specialist Review: Pass 1 for security review, Pass 2 for performance review, Pass 3 for correctness review. Each pass uses a specialized system prompt.
  3. Adversarial Review: Pass 1 generates a solution, Pass 2 tries to break it (find edge cases, security holes, failure modes), Pass 3 fixes the issues found.

Implementation

Multi-pass workflows can run within a single agent (sequential prompts) or across agents (each pass is a separate agent). Cross-agent passes provide stronger isolation — the reviewer can't be biased by the generator's reasoning.

Cost vs. Quality Tradeoff

Each pass costs tokens. For critical outputs (legal contracts, security configurations, public-facing content), the cost is justified. For internal, low-stakes content, a single pass may suffice.

exercises.question: Your agent generates infrastructure-as-code (Terraform) for client deployments. A recent deployment failed because the generated code had a security group that allowed unrestricted inbound access (0.0.0.0/0 on port 22). The generation prompt includes security guidelines, but they were missed. What is the BEST workflow improvement?
options: Run `terraform validate` after generation and trust it to catch security issues
Add more security guidelines to the generation prompt
Have a human review every generated Terraform file before deployment

Implement a multi-pass workflow: (1) generate Terraform, (2) security review pass checking for common misconfigurations (open ports, missing encryption, overly permissive IAM), (3) revise to fix any issues found
exercises.explanation: Multi-pass with a dedicated security review pass is the best approach. The security review pass uses a specialized prompt focused only on security misconfigurations — it's more likely to catch issues than the generation prompt which juggles many concerns. More guidelines in the generation prompt (A) already failed. terraform validate (C) checks syntax, not security. Human review (D) is expensive and doesn't scale. The multi-pass approach automates the security check as a reliable gate.

exercises.question: In an adversarial review workflow, why should the review pass be done by a SEPARATE agent rather than the same agent that generated the content?
options: The API requires separate agents for multi-pass workflows
Separate agents are faster because they run in parallel
Separate agents use less total tokens
The generating agent has its reasoning in context and may be biased toward defending its own output. A fresh agent reviews without that bias
exercises.explanation: The generator has its own reasoning in the conversation history and may unconsciously defend its choices rather than critically evaluating them. A separate agent approaches the output fresh, without the generator's justifications in context, making it more likely to identify issues. This is analogous to why code review should be done by someone other than the author.

exercises.question: You have a 3-pass review workflow for generating customer-facing email content: (1) generate, (2) review for tone and accuracy, (3) revise. The workflow works well but costs $0.15 per email. Your company sends 10,000 automated emails per month. Your manager asks you to reduce costs without sacrificing quality for important emails. What do you recommend?
options: Use the Batch API for all 3 passes — saves 50% with no quality change
Both B and C — classify by importance AND use batch for non-urgent emails
Eliminate the review pass for all emails to cut costs by 33%

Classify emails by importance: high-importance emails (contract renewals, escalations) get the full 3-pass treatment; low-importance emails (status updates, confirmations) get single-pass generation only
exercises.explanation: Combine both strategies. Classification by importance (B) reduces the number of emails going through expensive multi-pass review. Batch API (C) saves 50% on the emails that aren't time-sensitive. Together: important emails get full review at 50% cost via batch (unless urgent), and low-importance emails get single-pass at 50% cost via batch. Maximum savings, no quality compromise on emails that matter.

lessons.title: Programmatic vs. Prompt-Based Enforcement
lessons.description: Apply the decision framework for choosing programmatic enforcement (schemas, hooks, code) vs. prompt-based guidance, and implement defense in depth.

sections.content:
Programmatic vs. Prompt-Based Enforcement

This is the single most tested concept on the exam: knowing when to use programmatic enforcement (schemas, hooks, code) versus prompt-based guidance (instructions, examples, system prompts).

Programmatic Enforcement

Deterministic. Always runs. Cannot be bypassed by the model.

Mechanisms: JSON schemas, PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks, forced tool_choice, validation-retry loops, code-level checks in tool implementations.

Use for: security requirements, compliance requirements, data format requirements, business-critical logic, any requirement where violation has serious consequences.

Prompt-Based Guidance

Probabilistic. Usually followed. Can be missed under certain conditions.

Mechanisms: System prompts, user instructions, few-shot examples, CLAUDE.md rules, custom command instructions.

Use for: style preferences, soft guidelines, contextual decisions, creative direction, exploratory tasks.

The Decision Framework

  1. What happens if violated? Security breach/data loss/compliance failure -> Programmatic. Inconsistent style/suboptimal code -> Prompt.
  2. Objective or subjective? Objective (parameterized SQL, valid JSON) -> Programmatic. Subjective (readable code, clear naming) -> Prompt.
  3. Can you write a deterministic check? Yes -> Programmatic. No -> Prompt.

Defense in Depth

The best systems use both: prompts to reduce the frequency of violations, and programmatic enforcement to catch any that slip through. This is the ideal answer on the exam when both options are available.

exercises.question: Your agent generates database migration scripts. A critical requirement: migrations must never contain DROP TABLE statements in production. An engineer adds 'Never generate DROP TABLE statements' to the system prompt. Is this sufficient?
options: No — instead of a hook, add a PostToolUse hook that checks the generated SQL after execution
No — replace the prompt with a validation-retry loop that rejects SQL containing DROP TABLE
Yes — Claude follows system prompt instructions reliably
No — add a PreToolUse hook that scans SQL for DROP TABLE and blocks execution, plus keep the prompt instruction as an additional layer
exercises.explanation: PreToolUse hook is essential: it blocks the destructive SQL BEFORE it executes. PostToolUse (C) checks AFTER execution — the table is already dropped. A validation-retry loop (D) catches it but is more complex than a simple hook. The prompt alone (A) is probabilistic guidance for a critical safety requirement. The correct answer uses both the hook (enforcement) and the prompt (guidance) for defense in depth.

exercises.question: Which of these requirements should use prompt-based guidance rather than programmatic enforcement?
options: All API responses must include a request_id field
User-uploaded files must be scanned for malware before storage
Authentication tokens must be validated before processing any request
Generated code should prefer descriptive variable names over abbreviations
exercises.explanation: Variable naming is a style preference — it's subjective, non-critical if violated, and can't easily be checked deterministically (what counts as 'descriptive' is judgment-based). The other three are objective, security-relevant requirements with deterministic checks: schema validation for request_id (A), code-level auth checks (C), and automated malware scanning (D).

exercises.question:
You're reviewing an agent architecture. The system has these controls:
- System prompt: 'Always use HTTPS URLs, never HTTP'
- System prompt: 'Validate all email addresses before sending'
- PreToolUse hook: Blocks any Bash command containing `rm -rf /`
- inputSchema: Requires `recipient_email` field to match email regex pattern
- No validation on URL format in any schema or hook

Which security gap is MOST critical?
options: The email schema regex might be too strict and reject valid addresses
The HTTPS requirement is only enforced via prompt — there's no programmatic check. An HTTP URL could slip through, causing data to be transmitted unencrypted
The rm -rf hook is too narrow — it should block all rm commands
The email validation is redundant (both prompt and schema)
exercises.explanation: HTTPS enforcement is security-critical but relies solely on a prompt instruction — there's no hook or schema validation for URLs. Claude might generate an HTTP URL under certain conditions, transmitting sensitive data unencrypted. This is the exam pattern: identify the security requirement that lacks programmatic enforcement. The email validation (A) being redundant is actually good (defense in depth). The rm hook (C) might need broadening but at least has some programmatic enforcement.

courses.title: Domain 5 — Context Management
courses.description: Master context preservation, escalation patterns, error propagation, codebase exploration, human review workflows, and the lost-in-the-middle problem. Covers 15% of the Certified Architect exam.

lessons.title: Context Preservation & Compression
lessons.description: Mitigate the lost-in-the-middle problem and design context that survives compression in long-running agent sessions.

sections.content:
Context Preservation & Compression

Context management is about ensuring Claude has the right information at the right time, structured so that critical details survive context window pressure.

The Lost-in-the-Middle Problem

Research shows that LLMs pay more attention to information at the beginning and end of the context window, and less attention to information in the middle. This means critical information buried in the middle of a long conversation may be missed.

Mitigation Strategies

  1. Front-load critical information: Put the most important context at the beginning of the system prompt or conversation
  2. Repeat critical constraints: State key requirements in both the system prompt and the user message
  3. Structure with headers: Use clear headers and formatting to make important sections visually distinct
  4. Summarize periodically: In long conversations, periodically summarize the current state and requirements
  5. Use structured formats: JSON, XML tags, and markdown sections are easier to parse than prose

Context Compression Survival

When context windows fill up, systems may compress or truncate conversation history. Design your critical information to survive:

  • Put constraints in the system prompt (rarely compressed)
  • Use tool results for factual data (structured, easier to preserve)
  • Avoid relying on information mentioned once in a long conversation
  • Re-state critical requirements near the point of use

Context Window Budget

For long-running agents, plan the budget: System prompt ~10-15%, Conversation history ~50-60%, Working memory ~25-30%. When the window fills, older turns get compressed. Design so critical information is in the system prompt (permanent) or re-derived from tools (refreshable), not dependent on conversation history (ephemeral).

exercises.question: Your agent processes customer support tickets in a long-running session. After handling 50 tickets, the agent starts ignoring a critical constraint from the system prompt: 'Never share internal pricing with customers.' The constraint is still in the system prompt, but it's now buried under a large conversation history. What is the BEST fix?
options: Start a fresh session every 10 tickets to reset the context window, passing the current state via a structured handoff
Add the constraint as a PreToolUse hook that scans outgoing messages for pricing patterns
Shorten the system prompt to make the constraint more prominent
Move the constraint to the end of the system prompt so it's closer to the active conversation
exercises.explanation: This is a critical security constraint ('never share internal pricing'). Prompt-based enforcement is failing due to context pressure. The correct answer is programmatic enforcement via a hook that scans outgoing messages for pricing patterns. Session rotation (B) helps with context window management but doesn't prevent the constraint from being missed. Moving the constraint (C) is a prompt-level fix for a problem that needs enforcement-level solution. The exam philosophy: use hooks for security constraints, not just prompts.

exercises.question: What is the 'lost-in-the-middle' problem in the context of LLM architectures?
options: LLMs forget their system prompt after processing too many user messages
LLMs lose the ability to process information after their context window fills up
LLMs pay less attention to information in the middle of the context window compared to the beginning and end, potentially missing critical details placed in the middle
LLMs cannot process information that spans multiple context windows
exercises.explanation: The lost-in-the-middle phenomenon means LLMs attend more strongly to the beginning and end of their context. Information placed in the middle — especially in long contexts — receives less attention and is more likely to be missed. This isn't about context window limits (A, C) or forgetting system prompts (D), but about attention distribution within the available context.

exercises.question: You're designing a system prompt for an agent that must follow 15 critical rules. The system prompt is 3,000 tokens. You're concerned about the lost-in-the-middle effect. How should you structure the rules?
options: Put the 5 most critical rules at the very beginning, 5 in the middle, and 5 at the end — prioritized by importance
Spread the rules throughout the system prompt, each near the relevant context
List all 15 rules in a single numbered list in the middle of the system prompt
Put ALL 15 rules at the very beginning of the system prompt, before any other content, using clear formatting (headers, bullet points)
exercises.explanation: Front-loading all critical rules ensures maximum attention. Placing them at the beginning of the system prompt, before other content, leverages the primacy effect. Clear formatting (headers, bullets) makes them visually distinct and easier to locate. Spreading rules throughout (D) risks some getting lost. Splitting by importance (B) still puts some critical rules in the low-attention middle zone.

lessons.title: Error Propagation & Recovery
lessons.description: Design error propagation paths in multi-agent systems with retry, fallback, degradation, and escalation strategies.

sections.content:
Error Propagation & Recovery

Error propagation determines how failures flow through a multi-agent system. Good error propagation means failures are visible, actionable, and don't cascade silently.

Error Propagation Principles

  1. Fail loudly: Never swallow errors. If a tool fails, the error must be visible to the agent and, ultimately, to the user or monitoring system.
  2. Fail with context: Include enough information to diagnose and fix the error.
  3. Fail at the right level: Tool-level errors handled by the agent. Agent-level errors handled by the coordinator. System-level errors trigger monitoring alerts.
  4. Fail gracefully: When possible, provide partial results with clear indication of what's missing.

Recovery Strategies

  1. Retry with backoff: For transient errors (network timeouts, rate limits). Include a maximum retry count.
  2. Fallback: When the primary approach fails, try an alternative.
  3. Degradation: Continue with reduced capability when a component fails.
  4. Escalation: When automated recovery fails, escalate to a human with full context.

Error Propagation in Multi-Agent Systems

  • Subagent errors should propagate to the coordinator, not to the user directly
  • The coordinator decides whether to retry, fall back, or escalate
  • Each agent should handle errors at its level before propagating
  • Error context should accumulate as it propagates (don't lose details)

exercises.question: A coordinator spawns 4 subagents for a code review. Subagent 3 encounters a rate limit from the API and retries 3 times before giving up. It returns an error to the coordinator. What should the coordinator do?
options: Fail the entire review and report that it couldn't complete
Return results from the 3 successful subagents, clearly indicate that subagent 3's review (e.g., performance analysis) is missing due to rate limiting, and suggest the user retry that portion later
Retry subagent 3 immediately with the same parameters
Ignore the error and return results from the 3 successful subagents as a complete review
exercises.explanation: Graceful degradation with transparency. Return what you have (3/4 reviews), clearly flag what's missing and why, and suggest a recovery action. Failing entirely (A) wastes the 3 successful reviews. Silent partial results (B) hides the gap. Immediate retry (D) will likely hit the same rate limit. The user gets maximum value with full awareness of what was and wasn't checked.

exercises.question: When should an agent escalate an error to a human rather than attempting automated recovery?
options: When automated recovery has been attempted and failed, or when the error involves ambiguous data that requires human judgment to resolve correctly
Never — agents should always handle errors autonomously
Only for errors that occur more than 10 times
On any error, always escalate immediately
exercises.explanation: Escalate after automated recovery fails or when human judgment is needed. Some errors are ambiguous — for example, conflicting data from two sources that requires domain expertise to resolve. Agents should attempt recovery first (A is too aggressive), but should not try forever (C is unrealistic). Frequency (D) is not the right criterion — a single critical error may warrant immediate escalation.

exercises.question: Your agent calls a database tool that returns: `{"error": "Connection refused"}`. The agent responds to the user: 'I encountered a database connection error (connection refused). This usually means the database server is down or unreachable.' However, your monitoring shows the database is running fine. What is the MOST LIKELY issue and the BEST debugging approach?
options: The connection string or credentials in the tool configuration are incorrect. Check the tool's database connection parameters (host, port, credentials) in the MCP server or tool config
The database has too many connections. Increase the connection pool size
The agent is hallucinating the error — ignore it and retry
The agent is using the wrong tool. Check if there's a different database tool it should be using
exercises.explanation: Connection refused with a running database usually means wrong host, wrong port, or wrong credentials in the tool configuration. This is a configuration error, not a database issue. The debugging approach: check the tool's connection parameters. This is a common real-world issue when tools are configured with environment variables that might be missing or incorrect.

lessons.title: Codebase Exploration Strategies
lessons.description: Apply systematic exploration strategies (top-down, bottom-up, search-first) to build codebase understanding efficiently within context budgets.

sections.content:
Codebase Exploration Strategies

Agents exploring unfamiliar codebases need systematic strategies to build understanding efficiently without wasting context window tokens on irrelevant files.

Exploration Strategies

  1. Top-down: Start with project root — read README, package.json/pyproject.toml, project config. Then explore directory structure. Then dive into specific areas. Best for getting a broad understanding before going deep.
  2. Bottom-up: Start from the specific file or function you need to understand. Trace its imports, callers, and dependencies outward. Best when you know what you're looking for.
  3. Search-first: Use grep/search to find relevant files based on keywords, function names, or patterns. Skip irrelevant files entirely. Best for targeted investigations.

Context Budget Management

Every file read consumes context window tokens. Be efficient:

  • Read file structure (ls, glob) before reading file contents
  • Read specific line ranges, not entire files
  • Use search to find the exact location before reading
  • Stop exploring when you have enough information to proceed

Information Provenance

When an agent makes claims about the codebase, those claims should be traceable to specific files and line numbers. Best practices: reference file paths and line numbers, distinguish between information read from code vs. inferred, state uncertainty explicitly, re-read files before making changes.

Anti-patterns

  • Reading every file in a directory
  • Guessing file locations without searching
  • Making claims about code without reading it
  • Reading a file once and assuming it hasn't changed in a long session

exercises.question: An agent needs to fix a bug where the `calculateDiscount()` function returns incorrect values for bulk orders. The codebase has 2,000 files. What is the MOST efficient exploration strategy?
options: Read the README and project architecture docs first, then explore each module systematically
Ask the user where the bug is located rather than exploring the codebase
Read every file in the `src/` directory to understand the full codebase before looking for the bug
Use search to find `calculateDiscount`, read that function and its tests, trace its callers and dependencies, then identify the bug
exercises.explanation: Search-first + bottom-up: find the specific function, read it, read its tests, trace its dependencies. This is the most token-efficient approach for a targeted bug fix. Reading all of src/ (A) wastes tokens on 1,990+ irrelevant files. Top-down exploration (C) is valuable for broad understanding but overkill for a targeted fix. Asking the user (D) abdicates the agent's responsibility to investigate.

exercises.question: Why should an agent re-read a file before modifying it in a long-running session?
options: To consume more context window tokens for better accuracy
To refresh its memory because LLMs forget file contents quickly
Because the file may have been modified by another agent, a user, or a tool since it was last read, and changes based on stale content could cause conflicts or data loss
The API requires a read before every write operation
exercises.explanation: In multi-agent systems or long sessions, files can change between reads. Another agent may have modified the file, a hook may have triggered changes, or the user may have edited it externally. Making changes based on stale content can overwrite others' work or introduce conflicts. Re-reading ensures the agent operates on current state.

exercises.question: Your agent is exploring a new codebase and reports: 'The application uses PostgreSQL for its database, based on the import statements I found.' However, the project actually uses MySQL — the PostgreSQL imports are in a deprecated module that's no longer used. What information provenance principle was violated?
options: The agent's search was correct — PostgreSQL imports mean PostgreSQL is used

The agent made a codebase-wide claim based on evidence from a single location without verifying it against project configuration (package.json, docker-compose, .env) or checking if the module is actively used
The agent should have asked the user what database is used instead of investigating
The agent should have read more files to gather more evidence
exercises.explanation: The agent drew a broad conclusion from narrow evidence without cross-referencing. Good information provenance requires: (1) checking multiple sources (import != usage), (2) verifying against authoritative sources (config files, docker-compose), and (3) checking if the source is current (deprecated module). The agent should have stated: 'Found PostgreSQL imports in [file], but should verify against project config.'

lessons.title: Human Review Workflows & Escalation
lessons.description: Design escalation patterns (approval gates, confidence-based routing, exception handling) and package information for effective human review.

sections.content:
Human Review Workflows & Escalation

Human review workflows define when and how an agent should involve a human in its decision-making process. This is a critical safety pattern for production systems.

When to Escalate to Human Review

  1. High-stakes decisions: Deploying to production, deleting data, financial transactions
  2. Ambiguous situations: Conflicting requirements, unclear specifications, edge cases not covered by rules
  3. Confidence thresholds: When the agent's confidence in its output is below a defined threshold
  4. Policy violations: When the agent detects potential violations it can't resolve automatically
  5. Recovery failure: When automated error recovery has been exhausted

Escalation Design Patterns

  1. Approval gate: Agent proposes an action and waits for human approval before executing. For destructive or irreversible operations.
  2. Review + modify: Agent produces output for human review. Human can approve, reject, or modify.
  3. Exception handling: Agent operates autonomously for normal cases, escalates exceptions only.
  4. Confidence-based: Below-threshold outputs are automatically routed to human review.

Information Packaging for Escalation

Provide: the decision or action that needs review, the context that led to escalation, the specific reason for escalation, the agent's recommendation (if any), and all relevant data the human needs.

Anti-patterns

  • Escalating everything (defeats automation)
  • Escalating without context (human can't decide)
  • Not escalating critical decisions (agent overreaches)
  • Blocking on human review for non-critical decisions (slows the system)

exercises.question: Your agent auto-fixes security vulnerabilities in PRs. It finds a dependency with a known CVE and wants to upgrade it from v2.3 to v3.0 (a major version bump that may have breaking changes). What is the BEST workflow?
options: Skip the upgrade since it's a major version bump and might break things
Auto-upgrade to v3.0 since it fixes a security vulnerability — security always takes priority
Propose the upgrade with an explanation of the CVE severity, potential breaking changes from the v3.0 changelog, and a recommendation. Present to a human for approval before applying
Downgrade to v1.x which doesn't have the CVE
exercises.explanation: Major version bumps with potential breaking changes require human judgment. The agent should: (1) identify the vulnerability, (2) research the upgrade implications, (3) package all this information, and (4) present it for human decision. Auto-upgrading (A) risks breaking the application. Skipping (B) leaves the vulnerability. Downgrading (D) likely introduces other issues. This is a textbook approval gate pattern.

exercises.question: What information should an agent include when escalating a decision to a human reviewer?
options: The complete conversation history so the human can trace every step
The decision needed, the context that led to the escalation, the specific reason the agent can't decide, the agent's recommendation with reasoning, and all relevant data
A summary of what the agent did, without including its recommendation to avoid biasing the human
Only the question: 'Should I proceed?'
exercises.explanation: Comprehensive escalation packaging enables informed decisions. The human needs: what decision is needed, why the agent is escalating, what the agent recommends (with reasoning — this helps, not biases), and all relevant data. A bare question (A) provides no context. Full conversation history (C) is too much noise. Omitting the recommendation (D) wastes the agent's analysis.

exercises.question: Your automated code review agent escalates 60% of PRs to human review, creating a backlog. The team is overwhelmed and starting to rubber-stamp approvals. How should you tune the system?
options: Hire more reviewers to handle the volume

Analyze the 60% of escalated PRs to find patterns: if most escalations are for the same low-risk reasons (e.g., style issues, minor refactors), add rules to handle those automatically. Reserve escalation for genuinely ambiguous or high-risk situations
Disable human review entirely to eliminate the backlog
Increase the confidence threshold so fewer PRs are escalated, accepting higher risk
exercises.explanation: The high escalation rate suggests the agent is escalating cases it could handle automatically. Analyze the patterns: if 40% of escalations are for style issues, add style rules so the agent handles those without escalation. This reduces volume while maintaining escalation for truly ambiguous cases. Disabling review (A) removes a safety net. More reviewers (C) treats symptoms. Blindly raising the threshold (D) may miss genuine issues.

lessons.title: Context Architecture for Production Systems
lessons.description: Design four-layer context architectures (permanent, semi-permanent, dynamic, on-demand) that maintain reliability under context pressure.

sections.content:
Context Architecture for Production Systems

Designing context architecture for production Claude deployments requires balancing information completeness, token efficiency, and reliability.

Context Layering Strategy

Layer 1 — System Prompt (Permanent Context): Always present, never compressed. Contains identity, constraints, security rules, output format requirements. Keep concise. Budget: 10-15% of context window.

Layer 2 — Session Context (Semi-permanent): Project information, user preferences, session goals. Loaded at session start, persists through the session. Budget: 15-20%.

Layer 3 — Working Context (Dynamic): Current task information, recent tool results, file contents. Changes with every turn. Most likely to be compressed. Budget: 50-60%.

Layer 4 — Retrieved Context (On-demand): Pulled from tools, databases, or files when needed. Not stored unless needed for follow-up. Refreshable at any time.

Design Principles

  1. Critical info in Layer 1: Security constraints, output format, identity must survive any context pressure
  2. Refreshable over memorable: Prefer re-reading a file (Layer 4) over relying on a previous read in conversation history (Layer 3)
  3. Structure for scanning: Use XML tags, JSON, markdown headers. Claude can locate structured information faster than prose
  4. Separate concerns: Don't mix security constraints with coding style preferences
  5. Test under pressure: Verify your system works correctly when the context window is 80%+ full

exercises.question: You're designing the context architecture for a customer service agent that handles 50+ turn conversations. By turn 40, the agent starts giving inconsistent answers because it's lost track of the customer's earlier statements. What is the BEST architectural solution?
options: Increase the context window size to handle longer conversations

Implement a structured session state that summarizes key customer information (issue, preferences, previous resolutions) at each turn and places it in the system prompt or at the beginning of each new message, ensuring it's always in the high-attention zone
Limit conversations to 20 turns and force the customer to start a new session
Record the entire conversation and replay it at each turn
exercises.explanation: A structured session state acts as a running summary that's always in the high-attention zone (beginning of context). It captures key facts without storing the full conversation. Increasing context window (A) doesn't solve the lost-in-the-middle problem. Limiting turns (C) creates bad customer experience. Full replay (D) fills the context window even faster. The session state pattern keeps critical information visible regardless of conversation length.

exercises.question: Why should security constraints be placed in the system prompt (Layer 1) rather than in conversation history (Layer 3)?
options: Users can see conversation history but not system prompts
System prompts are encrypted; conversation history is not

System prompts are always present and never compressed, ensuring security constraints survive any context window pressure. Conversation history can be compressed or truncated, potentially losing constraints
System prompts are processed faster by the model
exercises.explanation: System prompts are permanent context — they're always present in full, regardless of conversation length or context pressure. If security constraints were in conversation history, they could be compressed or truncated in long sessions, potentially leaving the agent operating without those constraints. This is a direct application of the context layering principle: critical info in Layer 1.

exercises.question: Your production agent uses structured XML tags in the system prompt for different sections: <security_rules>, <output_format>, <domain_knowledge>. The total system prompt is 4,000 tokens. A new team member suggests converting everything to plain prose to 'make it more readable.' What is your recommendation?
options: Use JSON instead of XML for better compatibility
Remove the system prompt entirely and put everything in CLAUDE.md
Convert to prose — it's easier for the team to maintain

Keep the XML structure — it helps Claude parse and locate specific sections faster than prose, which is especially important under context pressure. Structured formats also make it easier to update specific sections without affecting others
exercises.explanation: Structured formats (XML tags, markdown headers) serve two purposes: (1) Claude can locate and reference specific sections more reliably than scanning prose, and (2) maintainability — you can update <security_rules> without touching <output_format>. This is especially important under context pressure, where Claude needs to quickly find relevant constraints. Prose (A) is harder to scan. JSON (C) works but XML is the conventional choice for system prompts. Moving to CLAUDE.md (D) changes the context layer and behavior.
